content:
# Joshua Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 226-227 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_002_page_001.jpg`

---

**1** And Joshua the son of Nun sent from the Shittim two men as spies secretly, saying, "Go, see the land, and Jericho." And they went and came to the house of a woman, a harlot, whose name was Rahab, and they lay down there.

**2** And it was told to the king of Jericho, saying, "Behold, men have come here tonight from the sons of Israel to search out the land."

**3** And the king of Jericho sent to Rahab, saying, "Bring out the men who have come to you, who have entered your house, for they have come to search out all the land."

**4** And the woman took the two men and hid him, and she said, "Indeed, the men came to me, but I did not know from where they were.

**5** And it was at the closing of the gate, when it was dark, that the men went out. I do not know where the men went. Pursue after them quickly, for you will overtake them."

**6** But she had brought them up to the roof, and she hid them among the stalks of flax that were laid in order upon the roof.

**7** And the men pursued after them by the way of the Jordan, as far as the fords. And the gate was shut after those who pursued them had gone out.

**8** And before they lay down, she went up to them upon the roof.

**9** And she said to the men, "I know that YHWH has given to you the land, and that the dread of you has fallen upon us, and that all the inhabitants of the land have melted away before you.

**10** For we have heard how YHWH dried up the waters of the Sea of Reeds before you when you came out of Egypt, and what you did to the two kings of the Amorites who were beyond the Jordan — to Sihon and to Og — whom you devoted to destruction.

**11** And we heard, and our heart melted, and there arose no more spirit in any man because of you, for YHWH your God — He is God in the heavens above and upon the earth beneath.

**12** And now, swear to me, I pray, by YHWH — since I have dealt with you in steadfast love — that you also will deal with the house of my father in steadfast love. And give to me a sign of faithfulness,

**13** and that you will keep alive my father and my mother and my brothers and my sisters and all who belong to them, and that you will deliver our lives from death."

**14** And the men said to her, "Our life for yours — unto death — if you do not disclose this matter of ours. And it shall be, when YHWH gives to us the land, that we will deal with you in steadfast love and faithfulness."

**15** And she let them down by a rope through the window, for her house was in the wall of the fortification, and she was dwelling within the fortification.

**16** And she said to them, "Go to the hill country, lest the pursuers encounter you, and hide yourselves there three days until the pursuers have returned. And afterward you may go on your way."

**17** And the men said to her, "We shall be free of this oath of yours which you have made us swear.

**18** Behold, when we come into the land, you shall bind this cord of scarlet thread in the window through which you let us down. And your father and your mother and your brothers and all the household of your father you shall gather to yourself into the house.

**19** And it shall be that anyone who goes out from the doors of your house into the street — his blood shall be upon his own head, and we shall be free. But anyone who is with you in the house — his blood shall be upon our head if a hand is laid upon him.

**20** And if you disclose this matter of ours, then we shall be free of your oath which you have made us swear."

**21** And she said, "According to your words, so let it be." And she sent them away, and they departed. And she bound the scarlet cord in the window.

**22** And they went and came to the hill country, and they remained there three days until the pursuers had returned. And the pursuers searched along all the way but did not find them.

**23** And the two men returned and came down from the hill country, and they crossed over and came to Joshua the son of Nun, and they recounted to him all the things that had befallen them.

**24** And they said to Joshua, "Surely YHWH has given into our hand all the land, and moreover, all the inhabitants of the land have melted away before us."

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscript
This translation was produced from direct reading of the Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A), dated c. 1008 CE, pages 226-227 (West Semitic Research Project, Bruce E. Zuckerman). Joshua 2 is written in the codex's characteristic three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The LXX Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, folio 164) was consulted as a secondary witness.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **Names — "Joshua," "Rahab," "Jericho," "Sihon," "Og"**: LOGOS renders proper names in their conventional English forms rather than raw transliteration. The Hebrew יְהוֹשֻׁעַ (Yehoshua) becomes "Joshua," רָחָב (Rachav) becomes "Rahab," and יְרִיחוֹ (Yericho) becomes "Jericho." This decision reflects LOGOS's formal equivalence methodology: these names have established English forms with centuries of usage in the English biblical tradition, and rendering them in their conventional forms preserves dignity and readability without sacrificing fidelity. Proper nouns are not theology; they are reference. The reader must be able to recognize Rahab when she appears in Hebrews 11:31 and James 2:25.

2. **הַשִּׁטִּים (ha-Shittim) — "the Shittim" (v. 1)**: Retained in transliterated form with the definite article, as the Hebrew has it. The toponym means "the acacias" and refers to Abel-Shittim (cf. Num 25:1, 33:49), the Israelite encampment in the plains of Moab east of the Jordan. There is no established English equivalent, so transliteration is the appropriate formal choice.

3. **זוֹנָה (zonah) — "harlot" (v. 1)**: The word zonah denotes a woman who engages in sexual commerce. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders πόρνη (porne, "prostitute/harlot"), confirming the straightforward sense. Some traditions have sought to soften this to "innkeeper" (following the Targum's reading of פונדקיתא, pundaqita), but this is without lexical basis in Biblical Hebrew. "Harlot" is the traditional English formal rendering, preserving the register of the Hebrew without euphemism or anachronism. The word "prostitute," while accurate, carries a modern clinical tone foreign to the narrative's dignified register.

4. **וַתִּצְפְּנוֹ (vatitspenno) — "and hid him" (v. 4)**: The Masoretic Text has a third-person masculine singular pronominal suffix ("him"), though two men are the object in context. This is a well-known crux. The singular suffix may function as a distributive ("she hid each one") or may reflect an earlier stage of the text where the object was treated collectively. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads αὐτούς (autous, "them"), a plural that resolves the difficulty. LOGOS preserves the MT reading "hid him" as received, noting the LXX variant. The singular is anomalous but not unintelligible.

5. **בַּחֹשֶׁךְ (ba-choshekh) — "when it was dark" (v. 5)**: Literally "in the darkness." The Leningrad Codex reads בַּחֹשֶׁךְ with the definite article implicit in the preposition-noun construction. LOGOS renders this as a temporal clause, "when it was dark," to convey the Hebrew's adverbial force in natural English while preserving the darkness reference. The gate would be closed at nightfall — Rahab's fiction places the spies' departure at the moment of closing.

6. **פִּשְׁתֵּי הָעֵץ (pishtei ha-ets) — "stalks of flax" (v. 6)**: Literally "flaxes of the tree/wood." The construct phrase refers to the woody stalks of the flax plant, laid out for drying on the rooftop — a common agricultural practice during the harvest season. The LXX renders λινοκαλάμη (linokalame, "flax-stalk"), confirming this reading. "Stalks of flax" captures both elements of the construct chain in readable English.

7. **חֶסֶד (chesed) — "steadfast love" (vv. 12, 14)**: This is among the most theologically significant words in the Hebrew Bible, appearing over 240 times. It denotes covenant faithfulness, loyal love, and binding kindness — the love that persists because it is bound by commitment. LOGOS renders it consistently as "steadfast love" throughout, following the tradition established by the RSV and maintained by most formal translations. The word "kindness" is too thin; "mercy" too narrow; "lovingkindness" (KJV) archaic but defensible. "Steadfast love" preserves both the affective warmth (love) and the volitional constancy (steadfast) of the Hebrew term. Notably, Rahab invokes chesed as the basis of covenant obligation — she has shown it, and now claims it in return. The theological weight of this term on the lips of a Canaanite woman is extraordinary and must not be diluted.

8. **אֱמֶת (emet) — "faithfulness" (vv. 12, 14)**: Paired with chesed, the word emet means "truth, faithfulness, reliability." In verse 12, Rahab asks for an אוֹת אֱמֶת (ot emet), a "sign of faithfulness" — a concrete token that the oath is reliable. In verse 14, the spies promise חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת (chesed ve-emet), "steadfast love and faithfulness" — a hendiadys of covenantal commitment. LOGOS renders emet as "faithfulness" in both occurrences, emphasizing the relational reliability rather than the abstract philosophical sense of "truth."

9. **הֶחֱרַמְתֶּם (hecharamtem) — "devoted to destruction" (v. 10)**: The Hiphil of חרם (charam), denoting the total devotion-ban (cherem) — the consecration of conquered peoples and goods to YHWH by means of complete destruction. This is a technical term of holy war in the Hebrew Bible. LOGOS renders it "devoted to destruction" rather than "utterly destroyed" to preserve the theological dimension: cherem is not merely military annihilation but sacral act. The verb carries forward from Deuteronomy's legislation (Deut 7:2, 20:17) and finds its narrative outworking in Joshua.

10. **נַפְשֵׁנוּ תַחְתֵּיכֶם לָמוּת (nafshenu tachteikhem lamut) — "Our life for yours — unto death" (v. 14)**: A compressed oath formula. Literally: "Our soul/life instead-of-you to-die." The men pledge their own lives as surety for the covenant. LOGOS renders this with an em-dash to preserve the terse, oath-like quality of the Hebrew. The infinitive construct לָמוּת ("to die") provides the stakes of the pledge rather than the content of a sentence, and the dash conveys this structural compression in English.

11. **חוֹמָה (chomah) — "fortification" (v. 15)**: The standard word for a city wall as a defensive structure. Rahab's house was built into the wall system itself — a detail confirmed by archaeological evidence at Jericho, where houses were constructed against and within the casemate fortification walls. "Fortification" preserves the military-architectural sense of chomah better than the generic "wall."

12. **תִּקְוַת חוּט הַשָּׁנִי (tiqvat chut ha-shani) — "cord of scarlet thread" (v. 18)**: The word תִּקְוָה (tiqvah) means "cord, rope" — from the root קוה, "to twist together" — but is also the common Hebrew word for "hope" (as in the twisting together of strands into something that holds). The scarlet cord is the sign of faithfulness (v. 12) made visible. The theological resonance between "cord" and "hope" is intrinsic to the Hebrew and would have been heard by the ancient reader, though it cannot be captured in a single English word. The LXX renders τὸ σπαρτίον τὸ κόκκινον ("the scarlet cord"), reading tiqvah purely as "cord." LOGOS follows the lexical sense ("cord") while noting the resonance in this note.

13. **כָּל־הַמֹּצְאוֹת אֹתָם (kol ha-motse'ot otam) — "all the things that had befallen them" (v. 23)**: An unusual participial construction. The Qal feminine plural participle of מצא (matsa, "to find") functions substantively: "all the findings," i.e., "all the things that had found/befallen them." The construction is rare and somewhat awkward in Hebrew as well. LOGOS renders "befallen" to convey the passive-experiential sense: events that came upon the spies rather than events they actively discovered. The LXX renders πάντα τὰ συμβεβηκότα αὐτοῖς (panta ta symbebkota autois, "all the things that had happened to them"), confirming this reading.

14. **כִּי (ki) — "Surely" (v. 24)**: The particle ki here functions as an asseverative ("surely, indeed"), introducing the spies' confident declaration. This is not a causal ki ("because") or a recitative ki ("that"). The spies' report in verse 24 deliberately echoes Rahab's confession in verse 9 — what a Canaanite woman knew by report, the Israelite spies now confirm by experience.

### LXX Comparison (Vaticanus)

The LXX text of Joshua 2 as preserved in Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, folio 164) was examined alongside the Leningrad Codex. The Vaticanus presents Joshua in uncial Greek, scriptio continua, with chapter divisions marked by marginal notations. The text of Joshua 2 is substantially concordant with the MT, with the following notable points of divergence or interest:

- **v. 1**: The LXX names Rahab as a πόρνη (porne, "harlot"), directly equivalent to the MT's זוֹנָה (zonah). No softening or reinterpretation is evident in the Greek tradition at this point.
- **v. 4**: The LXX reads αὐτούς ("them," plural) where the MT has the singular suffix וַתִּצְפְּנוֹ ("him"). This is the most significant micro-variant in the chapter. The LXX may preserve an older reading or may represent a scribal or translational correction of a perceived anomaly.
- **v. 15**: The LXX reads κατεχάλασεν αὐτοὺς διὰ τῆς θυρίδος ("she let them down through the window"), closely matching the MT. Both traditions agree on the rope-and-window detail.
- **v. 18**: The LXX renders תִּקְוָה (tiqvah) as σπαρτίον (spartion, "cord/rope"), confirming the concrete rather than abstract sense. The scarlet marker is τὸ κόκκινον (to kokkinon, "the scarlet thing").
- **v. 23**: The LXX's πάντα τὰ συμβεβηκότα αὐτοῖς ("all the things that had happened to them") is a clarifying paraphrase of the MT's difficult participial construction כָּל־הַמֹּצְאוֹת אֹתָם ("all the things that had found them"). The LXX translators interpreted rather than reproduced the syntax.

No variant in the LXX requires revision of the LOGOS translation. The MT as preserved in the Leningrad Codex is well attested and stable across the traditions.

---

*LOGOS | The Word, rendered faithfully.*

--- LOGOS
